casing.sentence_initial_capitalized.note: "In standard English prose, only the first word is capitalized" — Wikipedia: Capitalization

casing.proper_nouns_capitalized.note: "norm for recognized proper names to be capitalized" — Wikipedia: Capitalization in English

casing.days_months_capitalized.note: "names of months, days, and holidays always begin with a capital letter" — Wikipedia: Capitalization in English

casing.seasons_capitalized.note: "Seasons are uncapitalized (a hot summer) except when personified" — Wikipedia: Capitalization in English

phoneme_inventory.vowel_count_us.note: "There are 14 vocalic phonemes in General American English" — Wikipedia: General American English

phoneme_inventory.consonant_count_us.note: "There are 24 consonantal phonemes in General American English" — Wikipedia: General American English

phoneme_inventory.rhotic.note: "General American is rhotic while RP...is non-rhotic" — Wikipedia: Comparison of General American and Received Pronunciation

lexical_stress.can_change_meaning.note: noun PREsent vs verb preSENT distinguished by stress — Wikipedia: Initial-stress-derived noun

lexical_stress.compound_noun_pattern.note: "compound blackboard is stressed on only the first element, black" — Wikipedia: English compound

vowel_reduction.unstressed_vowel_becomes.note: "Reduction to schwa occurs in most unstressed syllables" — Wikipedia: Stress and vowel reduction in English

inflection_morphology.regular_plural_suf.note: "-es ending is typically used with nouns ending in...sibilants" — Wikipedia: English plurals

inflection_morphology.regular_past_suffi.note: "past tense is formed...by adding -ed to the base form (play → played)" — Wikipedia: English verbs

inflection_morphology.third_person_singu.note: "-es is added" after sibilants, otherwise -s — Wikipedia: English verbs

inflection_morphology.comparative_suffix.note: "words of one syllable require the suffix" [-er] — Wikipedia: Degrees of comparison of adjectives and adverbs

inflection_morphology.comparative_long_a.note: "words of three or more syllables require 'more' or 'most'" — Wikipedia: Degrees of comparison of adjectives and adverbs

articles.rule_based_on_sound_not_letter.note: "the rule is based on sound, not letter" — Wikipedia: A, an

articles.definite_article.note: "the definite article the" — Wikipedia: A, an

word_order.basic.note: "English is included in this group" [SVO] — Wikipedia: Subject–verb–object word order

word_order.adjective_position.note: "attributive adjectives normally come before the nouns they modify" — Wikipedia: Postpositive adjective

word_order.adposition_type.note: "English generally has prepositions rather than postpositions" — Wikipedia: Adposition

contractions.apostrophe_marks_omission.note: "apostrophe is commonly used to indicate omitted characters...in contractions" — Wikipedia: Apostrophe

contractions.common_in_informal_speech.note: "Contractions are common in speech and in informal writing" — Wikipedia: Contraction (grammar)

contractions.acceptable_in_formal_writin.note: "tend to be avoided in more formal writing" — Wikipedia: Contraction (grammar)

punctuation_layout.sentence_terminator.note: full stop/period "used...to mark the end of a declarative sentence" — Wikipedia: Full stop

punctuation_layout.words_separated_by_sp.note: "words would normally be 1/3 em-spaced" (space between words) — Wikipedia: History of sentence spacing

punctuation_layout.oxford_comma_standard.note: "no universally accepted standard for its use"; "house style will dictate" — Wikipedia: Serial comma

spelling_variants.color_colour_us_gb.note: "'colour' is the British...'color' is the American version" — Wikipedia: American and British English spelling differences

spelling_variants.ize_ise_us_gb.note: "American spelling avoids -ise...British spelling mostly uses -ise" (though -ize also used) — Wikipedia: American and British English spelling differences

spelling_variants.single_l_double_l_past.note: "Americans typically use...traveling" vs British "travelling" — Wikipedia: American and British English spelling differences

register_taxonomy.mainly_word_choice_not.note: "formality and informality being conveyed entirely by register, word choice, tone" — Wikipedia: Register (sociolinguistics)

register_taxonomy.contractions_signal_in.note: "texts that contain short words, contractions, and abbreviations are considered informal" — Wikipedia: Register (sociolinguistics)

forms_of_address.title_lastname_formal.note: "commonly followed either by an honorific and a surname, such as 'Dear Mr. Smith'" — Wikipedia: Salutation

forms_of_address.first_name_common_infor.note: "first names have become increasingly common in workplace environments" — Wikipedia: Naming in the United States

forms_of_address.maam_sir_service_contex.note: "'Sir', 'Madam', and 'Ma'am' are commonly used by workers performing a service" — Wikipedia: English honorifics

naturalness_translation.literal_translat.note: translated texts show "overly literal expressions that sound unnatural" via L1 transfer — Wikipedia: Language transfer
